source_paper_title: Weak Approachability of Convex Sets in Absorbing Games

context:
Page 4, Introduction (end of main contributions summary)

Weak determinacy for Big-Match games of type II remains a problem.
title: Establish weak determinacy for convex target sets in Big-Match type II
background_latex:
A (two-player, zero-sum) generalized quitting game has finite action sets split into non-quitting and quitting actions: player 1 has actions \(I=I^{\mathrm{nq}}\cup I^{\star}\) and player 2 has actions \(J=J^{\mathrm{nq}}\cup J^{\star}\). A stage payoff vector \(g(i,j)\in\mathbb{R}^d\) is associated to each action pair. The game repeats over stages \(t=1,2,\dots\); once any quitting action is played for the first time (i.e., some \(i\in I^{\star}\) or \(j\in J^{\star}\)), the game absorbs and from then on the same action pair (hence the same payoff vector) repeats forever.

A Big-Match game of type II is the special case where player 2 has quitting actions but player 1 does not:
1) \(I^{\star}=\varnothing\) and \(J^{\star}\neq\varnothing\),
2) \(J^{\mathrm{nq}}\neq\varnothing\) (so player 2 also has at least one non-quitting action).
Thus player 1 always plays in \(I=I^{\mathrm{nq}}\) and player 2 plays in \(J=J^{\mathrm{nq}}\cup J^{\star}\).

A (behavioral) strategy for player 1 is a mapping \(\sigma\) from finite histories of non-absorbing action pairs \((I\times J^{\mathrm{nq}})^{t-1}\) to mixed actions \(\Delta(I)\). A strategy for player 2 is a mapping \(\tau\) from histories to \(\Delta(J^{\mathrm{nq}}\cup J^{\star})\).

Fix a closed convex target set \(C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\). For horizon \(T\in\mathbb{N}\), define the (Ces\`aro) time-average payoff
\[\bar g_T := \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T g(i_t,j_t).\]
Let \(d_C(z):=\inf_{c\in C}\|z-c\|\) denote Euclidean distance to \(C\).

Weak approachability (in expectation) of \(C\) by player 1 means:
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \sigma_T,\ \forall \tau,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma_T,\tau}[\bar g_T]\big)\le \varepsilon.\]
Weak excludability of \(C\) by player 2 means: there exists \(\delta>0\) such that player 2 can weakly approach the complement of the \(\delta\)-neighborhood of \(C\), i.e. ensure the expected average payoff stays at distance at least \(\delta\) from \(C\) for large known horizons.

A game is weakly determined for convex target sets if for every closed convex \(C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\), either \(C\) is weakly approachable by player 1 or \(C\) is weakly excludable by player 2.
problem_definition_latex:
Prove (or disprove) weak determinacy for Big-Match games of type II: for every Big-Match type II game with vector payoff \(g\) and every closed convex target set \(C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\), show that exactly one of the following holds:

1) \(C\) is weakly approachable by player 1, i.e.
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \sigma_T,\ \forall \tau,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma_T,\tau}[\bar g_T]\big)\le \varepsilon;\]

or

2) \(C\) is weakly excludable by player 2, i.e. \(\exists\delta>0\) such that
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \tau_T,\ \forall \sigma,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma,\tau_T}[\bar g_T]\big)\ge \delta-\varepsilon.\]

Equivalently, determine whether there exist Big-Match type II games and convex sets \(C\) that are neither weakly approachable nor weakly excludable, or prove that no such examples exist.

The game repeats over stages \(t=1,2,\dots\); once any quitting action is played for the first time (i.e., some \(i\in I^{\star}\) or \(j\in J^{\star}\)), the game absorbs and from then on the same action pair (hence the same payoff vector) repeats forever.

1) \(C\) is weakly approachable by player 1, i.e.
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \sigma_T,\ \forall \tau,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma_T,\tau}[\bar g_T]\big)\le \varepsilon;\]

or

2) \(C\) is weakly excludable by player 2, i.e. \(\exists\delta>0\) such that

Equivalently, determine whether there exist Big-Match type II games and convex sets \(C\) that are neither weakly approachable nor weakly excludable, or prove that no such examples exist.
location_in_paper: Page 4, Introduction (end of main contributions summary)
open_problem_quote: Weak determinacy for Big-Match games of type II remains a problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The game class is finite, so candidate counterexamples can be searched over small action sets and low-dimensional payoffs, but verifying weak approachability/excludability is itself nontrivial and may require solving auxiliary limiting or differential-game characterizations rather than a simple finite certificate.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize small Big-Match type II games and convex targets and use numerical proxies (e.g., finite-horizon minimax with horizon-dependent strategies, discretized differential-game value approximations) as a fitness signal to hunt for candidate indeterminate instances or supporting evidence for determinacy, but the signal may be expensive and only approximate the asymptotic weak notion.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper of Flesch–Laraki–Perchet (2016) formulates the weak determinacy question for convex targets in generalized quitting games and analyzes it via three nested geometric conditions (1)–(3) built from mixed actions and nonnegative measures capturing “infinitesimal” absorption perturbations. Their main theorem shows: Condition (1) is sufficient for weak approachability (in expectation, horizon-dependent strategies), while failure of Condition (3) implies weak excludability. In Big-Match type I these conditions collapse to Blackwell’s classical condition, yielding weak determinacy for all closed convex sets. In Big-Match type II, however, the conditions separate: (1)–(2) characterize uniform approachability but are not necessary for weak approachability, and Condition (3) is not sufficient. The paper provides explicit counterexamples (e.g., Examples 16–17) demonstrating this gap and explicitly leaves weak determinacy for type II open.

Subsequent work summarized in Ragel’s thesis (“Approachability and constant payoff in stochastic games”) develops the approachability side further for absorbing games: it proves that a max–min–inf–sup criterion (Condition S, in the sense of Flesch–Laraki–Perchet) is sufficient for weak approachability in general absorbing games, and in Big-Match games (including type II) it is equivalent to weak approachability via block strategies. This yields more constructive sufficient conditions and strategy templates but still does not establish the desired dichotomy for every convex target set in Big-Match type II.

Other related developments (time-dependent Blackwell approachability; equilibrium existence in positive recursive absorbing games) provide techniques for handling time-varying evaluations or absorption-structure constraints, but they do not resolve the approachability-vs-excludability dichotomy for vector-payoff Big-Match type II. At present, no paper among the provided forward citations proves weak determinacy for all closed convex targets in Big-Match type II, nor provides a definitive counterexample of a convex set that is neither weakly approachable nor weakly excludable in that class. The problem therefore remains open and appears to require either (i) a new necessary-and-sufficient characterization of weak approachability in type II (possibly via an auxiliary differential game with a proven value), or (ii) an explicit type II counterexample exhibiting genuine weak indeterminacy.

sources.title: Approachability and constant payoff in stochastic games
sources.theorem_or_result: The thesis (summarizing Ragel’s MOR paper “Weak Approachability of Convex Sets in Absorbing Games”) proves: for any finite absorbing game with vector payoffs [57], if a closed convex target set [67] satisfies Condition S of Flesch–Laraki–Perchet (a max–min–inf–sup condition over [67]x[67]y[67] and nonnegative measures [67]\alpha\in M(I),\beta\in M(J)[67]), then [67]C[67] is weakly E-approachable by Player 1 (Theorem 70). It also shows that in Big-Match games (type I or II), [67]C[67] satisfies Condition S iff [67]C[67] is weakly E-approachable using block strategies (Theorem 90).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for weak determinacy for convex sets in Big-Match type II: for every convex [67]C[67], either Player 1 can weakly approach [67]C[67] (in expectation, finite horizon) or Player 2 can weakly exclude it. Ragel’s results do not establish such a dichotomy. They provide a sufficient condition (Condition S) for weak E-approachability in the broader class of absorbing games, and relate Condition S to weak E-approachability with block strategies in Big-Match games (including type II). The thesis explicitly states that “classical weak determinacy remains an open problem for Big-Match games of type II,” so it neither proves determinacy nor constructs a counterexample. Thus it is partial progress: it strengthens the approachability side (sufficient conditions/strategy constructions) but does not settle whether every convex set is either weakly approachable or weakly excludable in type II.

sources.title: Time-Dependent Blackwell Approachability and Application to Absorbing Games
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper extends Blackwell approachability to a time-dependent setting: given a closed convex cone target set \(C\subset\mathbb{R}^d\), a sequence of inner products \(\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle^{(t)}\) with nonincreasing norms, and time-varying outcome functions \(\rho_t\), if Blackwell’s condition holds then the generalized Blackwell algorithm guarantees for all \(T\ge1\)
\[
\min_{c\in C}\Big\|\frac1T\sum_{t=1}^T r_t-c\Big\|_{(T)}\le \frac1T\sqrt{\sum_{t=1}^T\|r_t\|_{(t)}^2}.
\]
It applies this to scalar-payoff absorbing games to construct \(\varepsilon\)-uniformly optimal strategies and reprove existence of the uniform value in absorbing games with compact action sets.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for weak determinacy (either weak approachability or weak excludability for every closed convex \(C\)) in Big-Match type II games with vector payoffs. This paper does not address Big-Match type II vector-payoff weak determinacy. Its game-theoretic application concerns scalar-payoff absorbing games and constructs \(\varepsilon\)-uniformly optimal strategies via an auxiliary 2D approachability problem. While the time-dependent approachability framework could be a useful technique for analyzing approachability in absorbing/quitting games, the paper neither proves determinacy for convex targets in Big-Match II nor provides a counterexample.

sources.title: Undiscounted Equilibrium in Positive Recursive Absorbing Games with Non-Rectangular Absorption Structure
sources.theorem_or_result: Theorem 2.8: Every positive recursive absorbing game that has no rectangular connected component of nonabsorbing action profiles admits an undiscounted equilibrium payoff (indeed a uniform equilibrium payoff).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns weak determinacy (approachability vs excludability) for convex target sets in two-player zero-sum Big-Match type II games with vector payoffs. This paper studies existence of undiscounted (uniform) equilibrium payoffs in multiplayer non-zero-sum positive recursive absorbing games, using a dynamical-system/finite-orbit construction based on the absorption-structure graph and joint exits. It does not address approachability/excludability, convex target sets, or the two-player zero-sum Big-Match II determinacy question. At most, its techniques about absorption structure and block strategies might be adaptable as tools in absorbing-game analysis, but no direct implication for weak determinacy is provided.
